replies.author: @tonyf9984
replies.text: This usually means footage stolen from other sources by AI. When I see this I report the video. OK, in this case not a big deal, but when a 'film' about Scottish islands mixes in footage from Iceland, it may well cause viewers to invest a lot of money in a trip to a place that doesn't exist.

comments.text: They don’t “belong” to Portugal, they are Portugal.  Continental Portugal also “belongs” to the Açores 😊

replies.author: @j.h.g.103
replies.text: No, it's not. Before we're Portuguese, we're Azorean. Continental Portugal is nothing like the Azores; we are Portuguese because of law. No Azorean feels like being Portuguese before being Azorean. If we could, we would be fully independent, but the mainland won't allow it. We produce more than we receive. Our way of life has nothing to do with Portugal—we just share a language, and that's it. We're Azoreans, not Portuguese! We may belong, but it’s economic, not cultural. If our economy and contribution to Portugal wasn't a net positive, we could be fully independent. Cape Verde was a Portuguese colony, but they are not in the EU and have their own national team... our politicians are bought... The first Portuguese President was from Faial, Manuel de Arriaga, with Teófilo Braga and Antero de Quental from S. Miguel... The Portuguese Republic was formed by Azoreans, and since then, we have been slaves. We pay four times what we get. If you say that, it's because you have never been here, because if you had, you would know that we're AZOREANS, not Portuguese. STFU!

I came here 10 years ago to stay.
Young people leave but immigrants from other countries replace them. And not only older ones! Families with small children too. Learning the language and working in different professions, paying taxes and blend in the communities. 😊❤
